themeBlurb: Paper 1 Writing: Situational (14m) + Continuous (36m). 50 marks total. Powered by Composition_Writing_Guide, ShowDontTell_KB, Register_KB, and ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.

composition.title: Continuous Writing — Narrative Craft
composition.blurb: 36-mark narrative composition. Story Mountain, F.A.S.T. characters, R.E.F. endings, Weather Contrast, Flashback techniques.
keyQuestions: Which technique fits this topic — Weather Contrast or Flashback?
Have I shown the emotion through Face / Action / Speech / Thought, not just told it?
Does my climax slow down to land the moment?
Does my ending circle back to the opening image?

glossary.definition: The five-stage arc: Introduction, Rising Action, Climax, Falling Action, Conclusion.
glossary.template: Each stage has its own pacing and emotional purpose.

glossary.definition: Show emotion through Face, Actions, Speech, Thoughts — not by naming it flatly.
glossary.template: Don't write 'she was scared'; show her clenched jaw, her trembling fingers, her racing thoughts.

glossary.definition: The reflective ending: Reflection (what was learned), Emotions (how the character feels now), Future-action (what they will do differently).
glossary.template: Image-anchored, not 'I learnt a valuable lesson'.

glossary.definition: Open and close with weather that mirrors or ironically contrasts the character's inner state. Three mentor essays use this technique.
glossary.template: Ash-grey sky → cleared blue sky as the character finds resolve.

glossary.definition: Open in the present (e.g., hospital, exam results), flash back to the incident, return to the present to reflect.
glossary.template: Signal with: 'Let me take you back to…'

composition.learnHTML:
Why this topic matters

Continuous Writing is 36 marks — 18% of the entire PSLE. The gap between AL2 and AL1 here is not about knowing more story rules; it is about showing emotion through body, action, speech, and thought rather than naming it flatly.

The Story Mountain (canonical 5-stage arc)

  1. Introduction: Establish the who, where, what — with a specific sensory anchor. No 'It was a bright sunny day.'
  2. Rising Action: Events leading to the problem; growing tension; character reactions.
  3. Climax: The high-tension turning point. Slow this down. Layer body reactions + inner monologue + dialogue.
  4. Falling Action: Tension eases; problem resolves; consequences play out.
  5. Conclusion (R.E.F.): Reflection + Emotions + Future-action. Image-anchored, not generic moral.

F.A.S.T. — the show-don't-tell lens

When you have written a 'telling' sentence ("she was nervous"), ask the four F.A.S.T. questions:

  • Face — what does the face do? (eyes widened, lips trembled)
  • Actions — what does the body do? (hands shook, feet rooted to the spot)
  • Speech — what is said or whispered? ("I…I can't," she stammered)
  • Thoughts — what runs through the mind? (every story Mum had ever told me about strangers came flooding back)

Any one of the four lifts a flat line. Two is AL1.

Three Band-5 mentor essays (in knowledge-base/ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.md) illustrate every technique. Read them once before drafting.

The Climax — where AL1 is won or lost

Most AL2 climaxes rush past the moment in a single sentence ("I was so happy when I saw her"). AL1 climaxes linger. Layer:

  • a body reaction (heart, breath, knees)
  • an inner thought ("It is over," whispered a voice…)
  • a sensory detail from the environment (light, sound, smell)
  • a piece of dialogue, if natural

Three to four short paragraphs at the climax beat one long one every time.

R.E.F. — the reflective ending

The R.E.F. ending refuses the cliché. Don't write 'I learnt a valuable lesson'. Instead:

  • Reflection: What did this moment mean? Define it as an image, not a sentence-shaped moral.
  • Emotions: How does the character feel now — settled, lighter, changed?
  • Future-action: What will they do differently? (Often left implicit — shown through a final image.)
composition.summaryHTML: Three Band-5 mentor essays in ModelEssays_Annotated_KB.md illustrate every technique. The Show-Don't-Tell library (ShowDontTell_KB.md) is the calibration target. Drill the high-frequency idioms in Idioms_Expanded_KB.md so you can deploy 1-2 per piece naturally.
pslePrompts: Open with a specific sensory anchor, not 'It was a bright sunny day'.
One sustained metaphor or motif per essay — set up early, return at climax, land in conclusion.
Use 1–2 idioms per piece, not more. Each must serve the moment.
End with a specific image, not a generic moral.
Numbers under ten: spell them out (first, fifth, ten — not 1st, 5th, 10).

questions.question: Which of these opening sentences best follows AL1 'show, don't tell' principles?
options: I was very scared as I walked into the dark room.
My knees trembled and I held my breath as the door creaked open behind me.
It was a very scary moment that I will never forget.
I felt afraid because the room was dark and quiet.
questions.explanation: Option B shows fear through Face (held breath) and Actions (trembling knees) without using the word 'scared'. This is the F.A.S.T. lens in action — body does the work, not narration.

questions.question: Which ending best demonstrates the R.E.F. (Reflection, Emotions, Future-action) framework?
options: I learnt a valuable lesson that day.
I never forgot that day.
As I rinsed the chlorine from my hair, the memory of his outstretched hand stayed with me. I would never look away again.
That was the best day of my life and I will always remember it.
questions.explanation: Option C anchors the reflection in a specific image (rinsing chlorine), conveys the emotion implicitly, and signals future-action ('never look away again'). Options A, B, D are banned generic endings.

questions.question: In the mentor essay 'A Day I Will Never Forget', the Weather Contrast technique works because:
options: The narrator is good at describing weather.
The bright sunshine outside ironically contrasts with the narrator's inner guilt — the world keeps shining while he suffers.
Sunshine is a happy symbol in stories.
The weather changes during the story.
questions.explanation: The Weather Contrast technique uses external weather to mirror or ironically contrast the character's inner emotional state. The mentor essay opens bright/inner-storm and closes bright/inner-truth — same external, transformed internal.

situational.title: Situational Writing — 14 Marks
situational.blurb: Functional writing across seven genres. Six content points (one is the underlined critical-thinking point not in the stimulus).
keyQuestions: What is the Purpose, Audience, Context, and Writer-persona (PACW)?
Have I addressed all six content points, including the underlined one?
Does my register match the genre (formal letter vs informal email)?
Does the salutation match the sign-off?

glossary.definition: Purpose, Audience, Context, Writer-persona — the four dimensions to lock in before writing.
glossary.template: Identify all four before drafting a single sentence.

glossary.definition: One of the six content points is underlined and NOT in the stimulus. You must invent a specific, realistic detail.
glossary.template: Don't write 'it is good'; write 'it reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg'.

glossary.definition: The level of formality — formal, neutral, or informal. Set by Purpose, Audience, and Context together.
glossary.template: Formal letter to principal: 'I am writing to express...'; informal email to friend: 'Hi! Guess what...'.

situational.learnHTML:
Situational Writing — 14 marks, ~25 minutes

You are given a SITUATION and asked to write a functional piece. You must:

  1. Use the correct FORMAT for the text type
  2. ADDRESS all six content points (one is underlined — that information is not in the stimulus; you must generate it)
  3. Use the correct REGISTER (formal or informal tone)
Missing one bullet point typically costs 2–3 marks. Missing the underlined critical-thinking point is the most common loss because it tests independent reasoning.

The seven genres (2025+)

  • Formal letter — to authority (principal, manager, council). Dear Sir/Madam → Yours faithfully; Dear Mr Tan → Yours sincerely.
  • Informal email / letter — to friend, relative. Hi [name] → Take care.
  • Report — to teacher, principal, council. Title, numbered findings, recommendations.
  • Speech — to schoolmates, parents, public. Engaging, persuasive; rhetorical questions OK.
  • Article (new in 2025) — to magazine/newspaper readers. Catchy headline, byline, opening hook.
  • Review — to readers. Balanced, opinionated; brief recommendation at the end.
  • Notice — to school community, public. Title in caps, clear concise points.

The 10-minute formula

  1. 1 min — Analyse: Identify PACW and the underlined point.
  2. 1 min — Number: Mark all six content points on the stimulus.
  3. 1 min — Decide: Finalise tone and format conventions.
  4. 7 min — Draft: Write, ticking off each numbered point.

The critical-thinking point — worked example

A task asks you to write to the principal sharing about a recycling programme. Five points are in the stimulus poster. The sixth is underlined: 'the impact of the programme on the school.' The poster does NOT tell you the impact. You must invent a specific, realistic detail:

  • Weak: 'It is good for the environment.'
  • Strong: 'We reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg.'
  • Strong: 'Two classes have started a compost garden using the food scraps.'
situational.summaryHTML: Situational Writing is 14 marks. Six content points to address (one underlined critical-thinking). Marking: Task Fulfilment 6 + Language & Organisation 8. The seven genres are detailed in Composition_Writing_Guide.md, with a full register matrix in Register_KB.md.
pslePrompts: Number all six content points on the stimulus before writing.
The underlined point is the most common one missed. Invent a specific, realistic detail.
Match salutation and sign-off: Dear Sir/Madam → Yours faithfully; Dear Mr Tan → Yours sincerely; Hi John → Take care.
No contractions in formal writing (do not, cannot, will not).

questions.question: Which salutation-and-sign-off pair is correct for a formal letter to a school principal whose name is Mr Tan?
options: Dear Sir/Madam ... Yours sincerely
Dear Mr Tan ... Yours faithfully
Dear Mr Tan ... Yours sincerely
Hi Mr Tan ... Take care
questions.explanation: When the recipient's name IS known: 'Dear Mr Tan ... Yours sincerely'. When the name is NOT known: 'Dear Sir/Madam ... Yours faithfully'. Option D is informal and inappropriate for a principal.

questions.question: The underlined content point in a Situational Writing task asks you to mention 'the impact of the recycling programme'. The stimulus poster does NOT contain this information. The best response is:
options: Skip this point; you cannot answer what is not in the stimulus.
Write 'the impact is very good for the environment'.
Invent a specific, realistic detail such as 'we reduced our weekly waste by 32 kg'.
Copy a different point from the stimulus instead.
questions.explanation: The underlined point is the 'critical-thinking' content point — it is NOT in the stimulus by design. You must generate a specific, realistic, plausible detail. Vague claims like 'very good' score zero.

questions.question: A formal email to the council should use which opener?
options: Hey there!
What's up? I wanted to say...
I am writing to express my concern about the rising number of accidents at the pedestrian crossing.
OMG you have to fix the crossing!
questions.explanation: Formal openers begin with 'I am writing to express / enquire / request / inform / propose...'. Options A, B, D are informal markers that lose register marks immediately.
